tagged_story_for_tts:
Ancient algae soup. Earth’s buried fuel.

The word oil comes from Latin oleum, meaning "olive oil"—but this black liquid isn’t for salads. It’s the remains of tiny sea creatures and plants, cooked by heat and pressure over millions of years, like a slow-cooker meal left underground for 100 million summers. When drilled, it doesn’t gush like a fountain; it’s sucked up through pipes, thick as cold honey, then refined into gasoline, plastic, or even lipstick.

A single barrel (159 liters) holds enough energy to power a car for 450 kilometers—or keep a smartphone charged for 57 years. Yet oil isn’t just fuel: it’s the invisible skeleton of modern life. The asphalt under tires? Oil. The synthetic rubber in sneakers? Oil. The fertilizer growing your lunch? Oil, again. Without it, the 20th century’s speed—jets crossing oceans in hours, factories churning out toys by the billion—would crawl like an oxcart.

Three related subjects are fracking’s hidden tremors, plastic’s ocean afterlife, and how solar panels outrun pumps.
